segments.text: today we are having a covert free zone believe it or not what do you think about that not going to mention the word again welcome to the aesthetics mastery show the show designed to help you raise the bar and thrive in your aesthetics career now on week three of lockdown am i right or is it longer I think we're coming into week four so what are we talking about today Miranda okay so we're gonna have a not Corona based show we're going to talk about lumps in lips so that complication can show itself in various different ways and how do we tackle it how do we overcome it and what can we do about it and we've also got a download where you get the download so if you go to the description below there's a link through to Tim's website and on this particular blog page there are various resources there's videos there's blogs etc and if just pop your email address in there you should be able to access those things that you can digest at your leisure jump

in now when I have my lips done I had a lump there and I just thought nothing of it and it did resolve within about after about two to four weeks oh my god but I just left it so what percentage of lumps and lips are actually just coming as a result of the needle and we should just disregard them so you're talking when you say a result of the needle it's a traumatic lump so it's to do with the trauma caused by the needle going into the lip and they are probably the most common I think which is simply that you get a little bruise cause a bit of information whenever you get blood in the skin it causes an inflammatory response we're designed that way so that we heal and that's a signal that there's trauma so white blood cells will rush to the area they'll clear up all the debris but obviously they are there and that means you can feel them so a lot of what you're feeling is the inflammatory response to trauma in the early stage lumps and lips now there are other cause as well which I'm sure we're going to get onto but that's when I think you're talking about so if I'm a customer and I'm freaking out because

I've got lumpy lips and I've never had lips done before just generally and now you know my partner might say to me oh I don't know I've gone and had your lips done you know they're all lumpy and then I send because when people do these pictures and kind of lift the lift back up and kind of pull it a bit it can cook look quite shocking and I send that picture to the practitioner what would you do if you received a picture like that it depends usually on the time that you receive it on so if it's the day you've done the treatment then I would reassure and say you need to give it some time it's way too early for me to be diagnosing a reaction or an infection or you know filler lumps that aren't gonna settle so the first thing is time is really important so many clinicians will go straight to the worst-case scenario doesn't matter when the actual reaction or problem happens so I mean this is similar with there with vascular occlusion in that if someone comes with delayed early refill they immediately think it's an occlusion where's but if you've got that plus a hematoma and you're on day seven and it doesn't look necrotic it's probably not

a vascular occlusion but that the time makes a very big difference to what the diagnosis is so the first thing is interpret the information that you've got in accordance with the time that it presents after the procedure you can't make an assessment without knowing how long it was since the procedure took place similar if you're gonna ask for help when this is one of the bugbears of I know I'm managing a big group with thousands of practitioners if you give the whole history but you don't say when the procedure actually took place it's almost impossible to make any diagnosis for almost anything unless the picture is barn-door so that's the first thing is interpret within the timeframe of when the procedure happened so if it was let's say a week plus and I'm still got these lumps what would you start to do so a week afterwards with lumps you're in the zone of starting to wonder whether this is either a reaction or an infection if they're getting worse because you should start to settle down within the first three or four days certainly if I'm getting to kind of a seven days afterwards and it seems like day seven

is worse than day six that is a red flag like you need to react quickly to that that's quite possibly an infection quite possibly reaction they're very hard to sell difference what it isn't is post procedural swelling because it's too late for that okay so how would I then begin to assess whether it's a reaction or an infection well the degree of swelling is important so really significant swelling a week after we once surveyed I think we had about hundred people and who answered the survey probably a bit more than that people clinicians who'd had their lips done and asked them how long after the procedure were they still getting swelling and we got 80% of people within four days where we've had minimal swelling but so there were a few people even up to two weeks but they were kind of getting to four for two you know one to four percent something like that who'd last for two weeks so the vast majority of people within a week are pretty much getting back to normal and so how can you distinguish between a reaction and an infection it's very difficult to be honest so the gold standard would be that you'd send a sample and had it looked at in the lab

but almost no one will do that with obviously not gonna biopsy someone stick something extra into their lips so you tend to make a judgement on clinical or treat it as both so the but that probably the biggest the single difference would be that if it's isolated in one place on the lip that's more likely infection so if you're reacting you'd think you've got filler it's reacting to your immune system is reacting to it why would it just pick on one place whereas an infection could just pick on one place because you might have just dragged in a little bit of bacteria with your injection or maybe they look they lip straight afterwards I'm always amazed that we all haven't got more lip infections because it doesn't matter how clean you are it's right next to the dirtiest part of your face so you'd expect there to be plenty of infections so I would look at the it being more I more focused on one point but having said that you could get infections in two places or affecting the whole of the top lip but you're looking for a focal point rather than a generalized reaction four foot to make the diagnosis of an infection okay that's super useful and then if it is

post procedural so just taking it back a notch to those people who have the swelling within that first week sometimes I know even from colleagues that could be quite shocking concept but it's normal but for them it's normal but or rather it's nothing to worry about but they are getting big ol lips so again how do you as a practitioner not freak out and think that's an infection or that's a reaction it's hard because sometimes I mean oh we I'm thinking of one person in particular who works for us who won't mind me saying but she and she swells more than average you'd say that and if she was a new client had never treated before I might more quickly treat her but we know the time course of it we've mapped it out and it's predictable if it ends on its own it isn't a true reaction cousin persist it's just a bit more swelling than average so that might give you one way of guiding but to be honest that the next category is it's partly around how the patient reacts so if someone is really upset on you know 24 hours later

I'd probably do what most practitioners do which is put some ice on it take some ibuprofen if you haven't got any problems it's trying and antihistamine if 48 hours they're still very upset and they've done all of that then I would step it up and I probably try steroids now I would at this point want to make sure that it's not an infection but I can be fairly certain it's not an infection if it started really quickly so within three or four hours all the lip area was swollen and I use my normal technique then I can say that's much more likely post procedural swelling and therefore I could confidently treat with steroids and but if you've got any doubt about being an infection you want to be very careful about using steroids because I could trigger or the other thing would be the start of cold sores mmm tell us about those so there's a you can trigger cold sores it's and it may be related to temperature this there's a common the exact mechanism now I did look it up once but you basically the neurons which aren't basically where the cold sores come from and they are a little bit more likely to start

producing cold sores if in changes of temperature and there's something around the procedure that seems to trigger this process trauma same as having dry lips or Sun on your lips um some people get a cold sore and the procedure will trigger them but that doesn't normally happen like the day of the procedure you'd normally expect it to build up over a day or two and then to peak at about seven days aren't they on to it so it's also a particular product achill time course but it's usually in one play at one place and we'll say it looks like a sore doesn't know as well so it's different to lumps and usually focused it's it is the cold sores are so often for some reason confused for when a vascular occlusion is going on I think don't want to face their worst fear and 24-48 hours afterwards when it's actually getting quite bad they will diagnose cold sores I've heard I've had so many cases I don't know where why it's so common but it's basically that you get little vesicles are formed with Nick with a necrotic lesion and so you've actually got little pustules forming and they diagnosed it as a cold sore when actually you've

gotten across a clip so don't confuse cold sores with um with necrosis and the way to do that is the pattern you normally a cold saw is on one place it doesn't break up along the pet along the path of the superior labral artery or the inferior label artery which is what so often seems to happen ok so tell me about infection how is that likely to present as a general rule infection after a wound it doesn't start immediately or you may get depends on that bacterial load but mostly with a clean technique you'll expect it to build up a little bit over the first 48 hours or so you expect the bulk of the infection to be a few days afterwards so it's very clear on day three to seven but it's probably not likely to show up on day one and it's also a little bit less likely to show up on day 25 and that would be more likely reaction so there's this rough time course which you know you can broadly expect but it's it is always worth keeping your eyes open to things that don't quite fit the mold because

nothing is exactly what you expect it to be and what they look like so is gradually increasing swelling usually with a focal point and quite often the filler chain it seems like everything changes texture you get a big immune response so if you actually feel people we've got an infection there there's often firstly a focus that you can feel much firmer and sometimes the filler is a little bit hard in there in the surrounding areas as well and you should be more painful should be swollen and you might if it's bad enough start to see some discoloration so you know a little bit of pustule forming but I would be very concerned about it being necrosis if you're seeing a lot of pustules for me and particularly if it's focused in one place so you'd rather have normal capillary refill with swollen red and maybe there's a pustule if you're unlucky but you wouldn't have a kind of darkish lift with delay camellia refill with some pustules that's a different diagnosis it's got bacteria in it but it's not the primary diagnosis would be a very fusion in those cases I think probably one of the

most common ones that I see on groups on Facebook groups who have off practitioners is these lumps that kind of are there a good few weeks later what are they you're much more in the realm of a delayed onset nodule so delayed onset lesions are probably caused by the immune system reacting to the filler in some way now the big debate is there a little bit of bacteria in there with it and there been some studies that have said yes there's always some bacteria in there with it and there have been others have said they haven't been able to isolate anything so one of the theories is a biofilm reaction my personal opinion is that it will be sometimes but those are more like an infection they tend to be localized in one place one lump that's troublesome comes and goes over a period of time that's more likely to be biofilm reaction if you're reacting to the product it tends to be everything going off at once and those reactions can be because your immune system is just hyper stimulated by something else or that you're just unlucky to basically react to filler so examples would be a flu-like illness any allergic reaction

generally you can you seem to be of people seem to direct some of the immune systems attention on the filler when they're fighting another illness so that that's the most typical thing is they're fine for a few months they get a runny nose you know a cough two or three days later their fill it all goes lumpy if you do nothing percentage of them will recover you don't need to do anything you can have antihistamines might help a bit ibuprofen might help a bit a percentage will go on and get worse and then you get this much more obvious response and they're important to treat because and this is my personal theory it may not be correct but a chronic long-term infection does make sense from the pathophysiology of information that you lay down collagen and then those lumps could be permanent so I don't want to leave people reacting with an undiagnosed lump for too long I'd rather treat them sooner to reduce the chance of long-term lumpiness caused by collagen deposition by the immune systems reacting to the filler how would you treat first thing is that

you can always give it a bit of time to see the directions going on if someone's got a very small you know they've got a cough or a cold and they've got a little bit of swelling and but there's nothing major they have got a temperature they're not seriously unwell they just have a bit of extra surrounding that lips you could leave that if they're not too bothered there's their home for a few days and just monitor it closely the next one would be if you've ruled out infection it's very important you real action before you start prescribing steroids but you could use steroids you could use antihistamines before that probably slightly safer going on from that if they are still reacting and maybe another reason I've used higher on days in the past is that they come down a little bit on steroids as soon as the steroids come back off they react again so in those cases you would probably I would probably dissolve it and it does depend some of this is you know that sometimes the client is desperate for it to work and for me personally my personal style is let's sort it out let's not let it brewing I don't like uncertainty with medical things if I can neutralize it with something safe rather than let it go on for too long I feel better doing that and so yeah but

that's there's a lot of practitioner kind of variation in that approach and then what about lumps that actually I think the public probably are the most aware of that the filler either hasn't been placed smoothly or it's is clumping itself afterwards what do we do about that and how do we know that how do we diagnose yeah so actually personally Mike's my suspicion is many of these pictures that you see that says oh the previous practitioner was awful and we saved them are quite often reactions or infections and certainly reaction or something reactive going on and there's a very typical shape you see where they get little lumps on the inside but know what no one really injects their I've had on one of my clients I never inject there there's something going on with a degree of reactiveness and maybe there's something Ellen with how the position the filler moves over time and but it's quite often that there's been some sort of um some sort of medical problem that then is dissolved and that does solve the problem then you can retreat and you

might be lucky it may not happen again if it was due to an illness that they were having but that's quite common I think injection error it does happen more commonly than bad injections because most people you know if you're bad at injecting you don't tend to get very busy so it tends to happen once you know I'm really not really enjoying yourself because you're getting these follow ups and you're Peter out and even doesn't matter medical nor medic like that's the kind of thing if you're not enjoying it you're probably going to stop you think or you're gonna get better at it so I don't see loads of problems a lot to do with bad injection more commonly is just semi bad injections there's not horrific this is not particularly sculpted or particularly well placed and it just doesn't look that nice yeah not crazy lumpy yeah there may be a degree of lumpiness but most lumps if it's just simple injection pad injection you should post a massage it out yes you know because we offer free consultations I quite often someone will come and see me cheekily kind of pop in for a follow-up from someone else done their treatment once they were in the waiting room and they booked in I'm normally massage you out and send them on their

way it's like a free service and when you were able to make how do you know this is appropriate to massage as a but once you've ruled out the other things yeah that's a good question so when you're examining a patient with a lump in their lip and firstly you want to see is this just one lump or is this multiple nodules all over the place because that completely sways you into a different territory and it does is there any sign of infection is it is the blood flow normally when was the procedure taken on all those diagnostic things you need to pull together and then when you actually feel a lump that's really helpful because a non inflammatory nodule will be should be fairly non-tender to squeeze you should have to squeeze in it should feel similar to the rest of the lip and those are the ones I'd feel a bit more confident giving a little bit more of a squeeze to so that they just dissipate and I'd say sixty percent of time they just blend in if you just squeeze hard enough and you're blending in you're not taking away the other celibates worked well are you so yeah it's moving what about different types of filler is the one that's more prone to lumps than another well so really it's really a difficult question to answer because mostly we don't have data on most

fellows so the FDA approved ones we do have some data and you could probably look up the ratio of lumps for those the rest of it you're relying on experience now I always think but I think that's how I first started and Hydra fill a CDS and it's we so long to actually get to know what that product was like because you don't what they all look pretty much I think they look the same on the day like give or take a little bit more definition with some products but mostly even if you inject say line you can give someone slightly bigger lips for an hour or two so it's really hard to get to know a filler based on what it looks like on the day and if you've been doing aesthetics for six months it's actually really hard for you to know unless you've been super busy you're seeing one or two clients a week or even one or two a day within six months you're gonna have a rough idea but you're not going to be able to compare with anything so what you get whenever you ask someone on a forum which filler do you like you get everyone saying that they like the filler that they currently use you know don't blame them because we tend otherwise you wouldn't be using it so oh I love it it's I would I swear by it get

beautiful results by it but it's not a test and you have no idea really by what people say unfortunately because that's actually what most of us are going on and what you have to lean on is your own ability over time to get to know that particular product and it's not even always about finding good products versus bad products as much as it is about knowing the properties of your product so that you can predict how it's likely to play out ok I think we've probably given you a pretty good overview remember if you want a really detailed look at everything we've spoken about and then you can go to the website how do you get there again so the description below there's a link through to brilliant page on Tim's website all about lumps ellipse ok thank you for watching take care [Music] you [Music]
